data.title: Supernus plunges on trial setback for depression therapy; Cantor downgrades
data.description:
Update 10.16 AM EST: Adds the Cantor Fitzgerald downgrade

Supernus Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:SUPN) lost ~20% in the premarket on Wednesday after the neuro-focused biopharma said its depression candidate SPN-820 failed to reach the main goals in a mid-stage trial.

The Phase 2b trial involving adults with treatment-resistant depression didn’t indicate a statistically significant improvement on the primary endpoint, designed to identify the reduction in depressive symptoms over four weeks through a clinical measure called MADRS.

In terms of secondary endpoints, there was no difference between the SPN-820 and placebo arms over the four-week duration of the treatment.

As for tolerability, the experimental therapy, however, indicated a safety profile consistent with prior clinical trials, and there were only a few adverse events, according to Supernus (NASDAQ:SUPN).

The company said it would further analyze the findings and decide on the future of the program after discussions with its partner Navitor Pharmaceuticals, a privately held biopharma based in Cambridge, MA.

Cantor Fitzgerald downgraded Supernus (NASDAQ:SUPN) to Neutral from Overweight and slashed its price target to $36 from $57 per share, removing SPN-820 from its model after the readout.

“After removing SPN-820 from our model, we believe shares are fairly valued to reflect the commercial business + pipeline,” analyst Kristen Kluska wrote, adding, “We applaud management for their transparency.”

data.title: Materials shine in 2025, outpacing all other sectors and the tech focused Nasdaq Composite
data.description:
Broader market averages are trending higher across the board in 2025, but the standout performer so far has been the Materials sector, which has outpaced all 10 other S&P sectors to kick off the year.

Despite ongoing concerns about inflation and tariffs, the Materials sector has shown resilience, advancing 7.5% year-to-date. This performance significantly outpaces the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND), which has risen just 3.2% over the same period. While the Materials sector typically flies under the radar, its strong start in 2025 is garnering attention as it continues to outperform amid market uncertainty.

As a result, Seeking Alpha formulated a list of the top 10 Materials stocks. The screened list covers U.S. stocks with a market cap of at least $10B. List is ranked by SA quant ratings.

For reference, Seeking Alpha's Quant system is driven by powerful computer processing and SA's special 'Quantamental' analysis. It awards grades to stocks based on collective value, growth, profitability, earnings per share revisions, and price momentum metrics.

Top 10 SA Ranked Materials Stocks

No. 10: DuPont de Nemours, Inc (DD), quant rating of 2.99.

No. 9: Corteva (CTVA), quant rating of 3.01.

No. 8: CF Industries Holdings (CF), quant rating of 3.11.

No. 7: Ecolab (ECO), quant rating of 3.21.

No. 6: Carpenter Technology Corporation (CRS), quant rating of 3.47.

No. 5: Eastman Chemical Company (EMN), quant rating of 3.53.

No. 4: Southern Copper Corporation (SCCO), quant rating of 3.54.

No. 3: Newmont Corporation (NEM), quant rating of 3.90.

No. 2: International Paper Company (IP), quant rating of 3.92.

No. 1: AngloGold Ashanti (AU), quant rating of 4.86.

Year-to-date price action: DD +10.5%, CTVA +13.7%, CF -2.4%, ECO +15%, CRS +21.2%, EMN +13.3%, SCCO +7.8%, NEM +27.6%, IP +6.4%, and AU +39.4%.

Furthermore, for additional exposure towards the Materials sector of the economy, investors can always look towards exchange traded funds for further exposure. See some funds listed below:

Materials ETFs: (NYSEARCA:XLB), (NYSEARCA:VAW), (NYSEARCA:IYM), (FXZ), (NYSEARCA:MXI), and (RSPM).

data.title: Business inflation expectations inch up to +2.3% in February - Atlanta Fed survey
data.description: Business inflation expectations at the one-year horizon ticked up to +2.3% in February from +2.2% in January, according to an Atlanta Fed survey released on Wednesday.

data.title: Expeditors appoints Daniel Wall as CEO as Jeffrey Musser announces retirement
data.description:
• Expeditors International of Washington (NYSE:EXPD) said on Tuesday that President and Chief Executive Officer Jeffrey Musser, plans to retire, effective March 31, 2025.
  • The board of directors has unanimously elected Daniel Wall, current president – global geographies, as the next president and CEO, effective April 1, 2025.
  • Upon his retirement, Musser will step down from the board of directors, and Wall will be appointed to the board and stand for election at the company’s upcoming annual meeting of shareholders.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Most shorted S&P 500 stocks in January
data.description:
Enphase Energy (ENPH) retained the spot for the most shorted S&P 500 (SP500) stock as a percentage of float in January.

As of Jan. 31, 17.16% of Enphase Energy’s (ENPH) shares were sold short, compared to 15.51% at the end of December. The energy tech firm had a short volume of around 22.22M shares at Jan-end, marking a 10.5% increase from the previous month.

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) followed next, with traders selling 91.66M shares of SMCI short as of Jan 31, or 15.65% of its shares float. Short volume in the artificial intelligence server maker grew 16% vs. December.

Moderna (MRNA) took the third spot, with a short interest of 40.6M shares, representing 11.84% of the float. This marks a 7.3% increase in short interest from the previous month.

Here are the top 20 most shorted S&P 500 stocks as a percentage of float, as of January:

data.title: Q4 e-commerce retail sales increase 2.7% Q/Q
data.description:
E-commerce retail sales for Q4 rose 2.7% from the prior quarter to $308.9 B, accelerating from Q3's 2.6% increase, the U.S. Census Bureau said on Wednesday.

Q4 total retail sales were estimated at $1,883.3B, an increase of 1.8% from Q3.

E-commerce sales for the quarter rose 9.3% Y/Y, while total retail sales increased 4.5% Y/Y.

E-commerce retail sales for 2024 were estimated at $1,192.6B, up 8.1% from 2023, and total retail sales rose 2.8% from a year ago. In 2024, e-commerce sales accounted for 16.1% of total sales, rising from 15.3% of total sales in 2023, the Census Bureau said.

data.title: STMicroelectronics upgraded to Buy on growth acceleration, iPhone 17 content: Jefferies
data.description:
STMicroelectronics (NYSE:STM) was upgraded to Buy from Hold by Jefferies due to expectations of revenue growth accelerating in the second half of 2025 and higher content in Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) upcoming iPhone 17.

Jefferies also increased its price target on the Netherlands-based semiconductor company to €34 from €23, or $35 from $24.

"Since Feb-22 we have had an Underperform or Hold recommendation on STM, with our EPS forecasts being consistently at least 30% below consensus due to concerns of a major industrial/automotive inventory correction," said Jefferies analysts, led by Janardan Menon, in a Wednesday investor note. "We believe Q1-25 represents the bottom of this correction cycle for STM's revenue and gross margin, with a small improvement in Q2 followed by stronger rebound in H2-25."

The stronger performance by STMicroelectronics will be due in part to a content gain of about $3 on the upcoming iPhone 17.

"We believe this increase is mainly from a meta-optics based 3D sensor solution for Face ID which eliminates traditional refractive lens components, allowing miniaturization and integration," Menon added. "We expect Apple to make a significant positive contribution to STM's growth in 2025, 2026 and 2027. STM's meta-optics solutions are also expected to be adopted in other consumer and automotive products."

Other contributing factors include a recovery in microcontrollers due to edge artificial intelligence apps; low earth orbit satellites and terminals, primarily from Starlink; and a new upcycle in auto and industrial semiconductors.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Wednesday: HIMS, NKLA, BMBL, GB, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged lower on Wednesday, the day after the S&P 500 closed at a record high, with investors navigating geopolitical and trade uncertainties while awaiting the Federal Reserve's meeting minutes for further policy insights.

Here are some of Wednesday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Global Blue (NYSE:GB) shares soared 17% after Shift4 Payments (NYSE:FOUR) announced a $2.5B acquisition deal, offering $7.50 per share in cash, a 15% premium to GB’s last close. The deal, expected to close by Q3, will integrate GB’s tax refund and currency conversion solutions into Shift4’s payments platform. Despite the expansion move, Shift4 shares dropped 9% post-announcement.
  • Appian (APPN) shares surged 24% after Q4 results exceeded expectations, with revenue rising 14.7% Y/Y. Cloud subscription revenue grew 19% Y/Y, while non-GAAP operating income reached $18.7M, reversing a $(1.4)M loss in the prior year, and adjusted EBITDA jumped to $21.2M from $1.0M. For Q1 2025, the company expects cloud subscription revenue of $97M-$99M (+12%-14% Y/Y) and total revenue of $162M-$164M, slightly below the $167M consensus, with adjusted EBITDA of $8M-$10M and non-GAAP EPS of $0.02-$0.05, above the $0.00 consensus. Full-year 2025 guidance includes cloud subscription revenue of $419M-$421M (+14% Y/Y) and total revenue of $680M-$684M (+10% Y/Y), aligning with the $683.09M consensus, while adjusted EBITDA is projected at $38M-$42M and non-GAAP EPS at $0.17-$0.22, topping the $0.12 consensus.
  • Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) shares jumped 16% after announcing plans to introduce at-home blood draws and whole-body testing through its platform following the acquisition of at-home lab testing facility Trybe Labs. The deal, funded with cash on hand, involves acquiring New Jersey-based Sigmund NJ LLC, which operates Trybe Labs’ services. HIMS expects to roll out at-home testing capabilities over the next year, with Chief Medical Officer Patrick Carroll highlighting the move as a step toward more personalized, comprehensive care, enabling providers to leverage richer data for improved clinical decisions

Biggest stock losers

  • Bumble (NASDAQ:BMBL) shares plunged 18% after Q4 results revealed a decline in Total Average Revenue per Paying User (ARPPU) to $20.58 from $22.64 a year ago, with Q1 2025 revenue projected to decline 7%-10% Y/Y to $242M-$248M, falling short of the $255.48M consensus; Bumble App revenue is expected to decrease 6%-8% to $198M-$202M, while adjusted EBITDA is forecasted at $60M-$63M (25% margin), as the company refocuses on product momentum, revenue strategy, and safety enhancements, discontinuing Fruitz and Official, and anticipates a short-term loss of 100K-120K paying users.
  • Arista Networks (NYSE:ANET) shares dropped 4% despite the company delivering better-than-expected Q4 results and guidance, driven by strong AI-related spending. For 1Q25, the company projects revenue between $1.93B and $1.97B, with the $1.95B midpoint exceeding the $1.91B consensus. Adjusted gross margins are expected to be around 63%.
  • Nikola Corporation (NKLA) saw its shares plummet by 45% after voluntarily filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in the U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the District of Delaware. The company also requested court approval to initiate an auction and sale process under Section 363 of the U.S. Bankruptcy Code. Despite the filing, Nikola plans to maintain limited service and support for its trucks in the field, including certain HYLA fueling operations, until the end of March, after which it will require external partners for continued support. Entering Chapter 11 with approximately $47M in cash, the company aims to fund ongoing operations, facilitate asset sales, and navigate the bankruptcy process efficiently. CEO Steve Girsky acknowledged the challenges faced by the electric vehicle industry, citing efforts to secure capital and stabilize the balance sheet. However, despite these measures, the company determined that Chapter 11 was the most viable option for its future and stakeholders.
  • Etsy (ETSY) shares dropped about 5% after the company reported mixed Q4 results, as weaker holiday spending led to a 6.8% decline in gross merchandise sales (GMS). While the company managed to reactivate 9.8 million lapsed buyers, the number of active buyers fell 2.6%, and spending per buyer declined 3.5% to $121. Despite these challenges, Etsy saw an 8.1% rise in services revenue, which helped offset a 1.4% drop in marketplace revenue, bringing total revenue to $852.2M—up 1.2% Y/Y but missing estimates by $9.2M. Profitability improved as cost of sales fell 15%, leading to an earnings beat of 9 cents per share. Looking ahead, Etsy expects continued GMS weakness in Q1, with declines mirroring the Y/Y trends seen in Q4.
